items.title: Build a CRUD system from Excel requirements to MSSQL
items.description: Objective: turn an informal Excel/CSV requirements workbook into a normalized, validated, auditable CRUD application on ASP.NET Core MVC + EF Core + MSSQL. Inputs required: the workbook(s); a data dictionary or the column headers; sample rows; the list of distinct statuses/lookups; named owners for each entity; expected record volumes and growth; who creates vs. approves data; retention rules. Analysis steps: 1) Inventory every sheet and column; classify each column as identity, attribute, foreign-key, lookup/enum, derived, or note. 2) Detect entities (a sheet often hides 2-3 entities); separate master data (Customer, Branch) from transactions (Payment, Request). 3) Normalize to 3NF: pull repeating groups into child tables and free-text lookups into reference tables with surrogate keys. 4) Define keys, nullability, lengths, precision (use decimal(p,s) for money, never float), and indexes for the columns the list/search views filter on. 5) Derive validation rules (required, range, regex, cross-field) and the status lifecycle. 6) Design view models per screen (List/Details/Create/Edit) selecting only needed columns. 7) Generate EF Core entities, a migration, and seed for lookups. 8) Define audit columns (CreatedBy/At, ModifiedBy/At, RowVersion).
items.applicability: Use when a client runs a business process in a spreadsheet and wants a real multi-user system with validation, history, and reporting; the most common 'rescue the Excel' engagement in banking middleware.
items.example: Outputs: an entity-relationship model; a column-by-column data dictionary with types and constraints; EF Core entity classes and one additive migration; seeded lookup tables; per-screen view models that never materialize large text in lists; a one-time import script that maps the legacy rows and a reject report for rows that fail validation; a smoke-test checklist for each CRUD path.
items.limitation: Risks: hidden business rules live only in the spreadsheet author's head; 'one column, many meanings'; duplicate/dirty legacy keys; money stored as text. Tests/validation: round-trip import vs. source row counts and control totals; boundary tests on every constraint; over-posting tests on Edit; concurrency test on RowVersion. Rollback/supportability: keep the original workbook immutable as the system of record until reconciliation passes; ship the import as re-runnable and idempotent; log every rejected row with reason. Executive decision summary: 'Spreadsheet X becomes system Y with N entities, validated input, and full history; cutover gated on a clean reconciliation report.' Assumptions: column semantics confirmed with the business owner. Human/expert sign-off on the data model and the reconciliation is required before cutover.

items.title: Modernize a VB6 desktop app to ASP.NET Core MVC
items.description: Objective: replatform a legacy VB6/VBA Win32 application to a supported ASP.NET Core MVC web application without losing undocumented business logic. Inputs required: the VB6 source (.frm/.bas/.cls), the compiled binaries, the backing database schema, any COM/ActiveX/OCX dependencies, sample data, a list of reports printed, and access to one or two power users. Analysis steps: 1) Inventory forms, modules, classes, and external dependencies (Crystal Reports, ADO, third-party OCX, Win32 API calls). 2) Recover the data model from the DB plus the SQL embedded in code. 3) Extract business rules from event handlers and modules; tag rules that exist only in code (validation, rounding, sequencing). 4) Map each VB6 form to a screen/use case and classify: rehost as-is, refactor, rewrite, or retire. 5) Choose a strategy: full rewrite vs. strangler-fig (stand up the new app beside the old, migrate one capability at a time behind a routing facade). 6) Re-implement UI in MVC + view models, data access in EF Core, and reports in a modern engine. 7) Define a parallel-run period comparing old vs. new outputs on identical inputs.
items.applicability: Use when an unsupported VB6/desktop app is a key-person and platform risk (no 64-bit, no installer story, OCX no longer licensed) and the bank needs a maintainable, deployable replacement.
items.example: Outputs: a capability inventory with rehost/refactor/rewrite/retire decisions; a recovered data model and rules catalogue with source line references; a strangler-fig migration plan with ordered slices; an MVC skeleton with the first vertical slice working end to end; a parallel-run comparison harness; a dependency-retirement list (which OCX/COM goes away).
items.limitation: Risks: silent behavior in VB6 (implicit type coercion, default properties, 1-based arrays, On Error Resume Next swallowing failures, screen-scrape printing) is easy to miss and hard to reproduce; pixel-perfect UI parity is usually not worth it and should not be promised. Do not claim automated VB6-to-C# translation produces production code; machine-translated output needs full review and is often slower than a clean rewrite of the slice. Tests/validation: characterization tests capturing current outputs before changing anything; parallel-run reconciliation on real transactions; report-output diffing. Rollback/supportability: keep the VB6 app runnable during parallel run; migrate per capability so any slice can be rolled back independently; document recovered rules so support no longer needs the original author. Executive decision summary: 'Unsupported VB6 app replaced incrementally; each slice proven by parallel run before the old path is retired; no big-bang cutover.' Assumptions: source is complete and DB is the system of record. Expert sign-off on recovered business rules and on each parallel-run result is required.

items.title: Design a maker/checker/approver workflow
items.description: Objective: design a segregation-of-duties workflow where one user prepares (maker), a second verifies (checker), and an authority approves, with a tamper-evident audit trail. Inputs required: the transaction types in scope; monetary/risk thresholds that change the number of approvals; the role hierarchy; whether self-approval is ever allowed; SLA/escalation expectations; regulatory drivers. Analysis steps: 1) Model the state machine (Draft -> Submitted -> Checked -> Approved / Returned / Rejected) and the allowed transitions per role. 2) Define authority limits by amount/type so high-value items require additional or higher approvers. 3) Enforce four-eyes server-side: the maker can never be the checker or approver of the same item; enforce by user id, not just by role. 4) Design the audit model: append-only, who/what/when/old-new values, immutable once written. 5) Define return/reject loops and rework rules. 6) Design notifications, SLA timers, and escalation. 7) Add idempotency and optimistic concurrency so two approvers cannot double-action.
items.applicability: Use for payments, limit changes, master-data edits, GL postings, and any banking action requiring segregation of duties and an audit trail.
items.example: Outputs: a documented state machine and transition matrix; a role/authority-limit table; an append-only audit schema; server-side guard logic that rejects self-checking; SLA/escalation rules; screen flows for maker, checker, and approver inboxes; a report of pending items by age.
items.limitation: Risks: enforcing four-eyes only in the UI (bypassable via direct API), role inflation that defeats segregation, delegation/out-of-office paths that quietly allow self-approval, audit records that can be edited. Tests/validation: attempt maker=checker and verify server rejection; test threshold boundaries; test concurrent approvals; verify audit immutability and completeness; test delegation does not collapse the second pair of eyes. Rollback/supportability: pending items must survive a restart; provide an admin view to inspect stuck items without mutating audit; design reversal as a new compensating transaction, never a silent delete. Executive decision summary: 'Every in-scope action requires independent preparation, verification, and authorization, enforced server-side with an immutable trail.' Assumptions: thresholds and authority limits confirmed by the business/compliance. Compliance sign-off on the segregation rules is required.

items.title: Design a dynamic report builder
items.description: Objective: let business users compose filtered, grouped, aggregated reports over governed data without writing SQL or risking the production database. Inputs required: the entities/columns users may report on; sensitivity classification per column; expected row volumes; required output formats (grid, CSV, Excel, PDF); refresh frequency; who may see what (row-level security needs). Analysis steps: 1) Define a metadata layer (semantic model) exposing whitelisted fields with display names, types, and allowed operators. 2) Map user-built filter/group/aggregate selections to parameterized queries; never concatenate user input into SQL. 3) Enforce field- and row-level authorization in the query, not in the UI. 4) Run reporting against a read replica or read-optimized schema to protect OLTP. 5) Add result caps, timeouts, and pagination to prevent runaway queries. 6) Provide save/share/schedule of report definitions (definitions, not result sets, are stored). 7) Add export with the same authorization applied.
items.applicability: Use when stakeholders constantly request new one-off reports from IT and the backlog is unsustainable, common in operations and finance teams.
items.example: Outputs: a governed semantic model of reportable fields; a query builder that emits only parameterized SQL; row/field-level security rules; runaway-query guards (row caps, timeouts); saved report definitions with sharing; multi-format export; an audit of who exported sensitive data.
items.limitation: Risks: SQL injection via dynamic predicates, performance collapse from unbounded joins/aggregations on OLTP, exposure of sensitive columns through clever grouping, large exports leaking PII. Tests/validation: injection tests on every operator; load tests with worst-case filters; authorization tests proving a user cannot reach unpermitted fields/rows; export-audit verification. Rollback/supportability: feature-flag the builder; cap concurrency; ship a kill switch and per-query timeout; log slow report definitions for review. Executive decision summary: 'Business users self-serve governed reports without SQL or production risk; sensitive data stays gated and every query is parameterized and capped.' Assumptions: a read replica or read model is available for heavy reporting. Data-owner sign-off on field/row exposure is required.

items.title: Analyze a failed import or OCR workflow
items.description: Objective: diagnose why an ingestion/OCR pipeline is dropping, corrupting, or misreading records, and produce a prioritized fix list. Inputs required: failed and succeeded sample inputs; the pipeline logs; the stage boundaries (intake, parse/decode, extract/OCR, validate, persist); error/reject counts per stage; encoding and file-type assumptions; throughput SLAs. Analysis steps: 1) Build a funnel: count items entering and leaving each stage to localize where loss occurs. 2) Classify failures: malformed/binary input, wrong encoding, schema mismatch, OCR low-confidence, validation rejects, downstream timeouts. 3) Reproduce on the smallest failing sample. 4) Check robustness invariants: does one bad file crash the whole batch, or is the per-item loop isolated? Is encoding detected or assumed? 5) Inspect confidence/quality scores for OCR and whether low-confidence items are silently accepted. 6) Verify idempotency and dedupe so retries do not double-post. 7) Quantify the blast radius (silent vs. surfaced failures).
items.applicability: Use when a batch import or document/cheque OCR job 'mostly works' but quietly loses or mangles records, eroding trust in the data, frequent in legacy banking intake pipelines.
items.example: Outputs: a per-stage funnel with loss numbers; a failure taxonomy with counts and example inputs; a reproduction case; a ranked remediation list (isolate per-item failures, honest encoding detection, surface low-confidence OCR for review, add dedupe/idempotency); a gap/coverage report showing what was skipped and why.
items.limitation: Risks: silent blind spots (items neither imported nor reported as failed), encoding guesses that corrupt text, OCR confidence treated as ground truth. Tests/validation: deliberately feed malformed/binary/zero-byte/oversized files and verify the batch never crashes and every skip is reported; reconcile input count vs. imported + rejected; verify retries are idempotent. Rollback/supportability: never crash the whole run on one bad item; always emit a coverage/gap report; keep originals so a fixed pipeline can re-run. Executive decision summary: 'Loss is localized to stage X (N records); fixes restore complete, reconciled ingestion with no silent gaps.' Assumptions: logs are sufficient to build the funnel. Domain sign-off on acceptable OCR confidence thresholds is required.

items.title: Design a banking reconciliation dashboard
items.description: Objective: design a dashboard that compares two or more sources of truth (e.g., internal ledger vs. bank statement vs. switch/clearing file), surfaces breaks, and tracks them to resolution. Inputs required: the source feeds and their formats; the matching keys and tolerances (amount, date window, reference); cut-off times; expected volumes; who investigates breaks; aging/escalation policy. Analysis steps: 1) Define the matching model: exact, fuzzy (date/amount tolerance), and many-to-one/one-to-many cases. 2) Classify outcomes: matched, unmatched-source-A, unmatched-source-B, amount-mismatch, duplicate. 3) Design control totals (count and value) per source per period as the first integrity check. 4) Design the break lifecycle (open -> investigating -> resolved/written-off) with audit. 5) Build aging buckets and SLA/escalation for unresolved breaks. 6) Define drill-down from a KPI tile to the underlying items. 7) Ensure the dashboard reads from a snapshot/read model, never blocking on live feeds.
items.applicability: Use for cash, nostro, card-settlement, cheque-clearing, or GL reconciliation where unmatched items must be found, aged, and cleared with an audit trail.
items.example: Outputs: a matching-rule specification with tolerances; control-total checks per period; a break-management lifecycle with audit; aging buckets and escalation rules; KPI tiles (match rate, open breaks, value at risk) with drill-down; an exportable breaks report for finance.
items.limitation: Risks: false matches from loose tolerances hiding real breaks, timing differences misread as breaks across cut-offs, dashboards that hang because they query live feeds synchronously (forbidden on the request path). Tests/validation: seeded test sets with known matches/breaks/duplicates; control-total reconciliation; boundary tests on date/amount tolerance; verify the page renders from a cached snapshot under feed outage. Rollback/supportability: reconciliation must be re-runnable for a period without double-counting; keep raw feeds immutable; never auto-write-off without human action. Executive decision summary: 'Breaks between sources are detected, aged, and tracked to closure with control totals proving completeness.' Assumptions: matching keys and tolerances confirmed by finance. Finance sign-off on tolerances and write-off rules is required.

items.title: Design a user-role-access matrix
items.description: Objective: produce an authoritative role-based access matrix that maps roles to permissions to resources, deny-by-default, and is enforceable server-side. Inputs required: the list of business roles; the resources/screens/actions to protect; data sensitivity classification; segregation-of-duties constraints; any project- or branch-level scoping; regulatory access requirements. Analysis steps: 1) Enumerate resources and the actions on each (view, create, edit, approve, export, delete). 2) Define roles from job functions, not individuals. 3) Build the role x permission matrix; mark each cell allow/deny with deny as default. 4) Identify toxic combinations (segregation conflicts) and forbid them. 5) Add data-scope dimension (which projects/branches a role may see) to prevent horizontal privilege escalation (IDOR). 6) Decide where authorization is enforced (server-side, on every action, including APIs). 7) Define access-review and recertification cadence.
items.applicability: Use when access has grown ad hoc, audit flags excessive permissions, or a new system needs a defensible authorization model before go-live.
items.example: Outputs: a role catalogue; a role x permission matrix with deny-by-default; a segregation-of-duties conflict list; a data-scoping model preventing cross-project/cross-branch access; an enforcement design (server-side checks on actions and APIs); an access-recertification schedule.
items.limitation: Risks: role explosion, UI-only enforcement (bypassable), IDOR where a user edits another tenant's record by guessing an id, privilege creep over time. Tests/validation: negative tests proving each denied action is refused server-side; IDOR tests with mismatched ids; segregation-conflict tests; verify API endpoints enforce the same matrix as the UI. Rollback/supportability: keep an exportable current-state access report for auditors; design role changes as reviewable, not silent; log authorization denials. Executive decision summary: 'Access is least-privilege, deny-by-default, scoped per project, and enforced server-side on every action.' Assumptions: roles reflect actual job functions. Security/compliance sign-off on the matrix and segregation rules is required.

items.title: Create a deployment readiness plan
items.description: Objective: produce a go/no-go deployment readiness assessment and runbook for releasing to a regulated banking environment (e.g., IIS on-prem). Inputs required: the build artifact and version; the target environment topology; config/secret inventory; DB migration list; dependencies (SQL Server, optional services); change-window and approval requirements; backout expectations. Analysis steps: 1) Confirm build provenance and that the exact artifact was tested. 2) Inventory configuration and secrets; verify none are in source and all exist in the target. 3) Review DB migrations: additive-only? backout path? data volume/locking risk during apply. 4) Verify the app degrades gracefully if optional services are absent. 5) Check health endpoints, logging, and timing middleware are in place. 6) Define the deployment sequence, smoke tests, and explicit rollback steps. 7) Confirm change approval, change window, and on-call coverage. 8) Build a go/no-go checklist with named owners.
items.applicability: Use before any production release to a controlled environment where downtime, data loss, or a failed change has regulatory and customer impact.
items.example: Outputs: a go/no-go readiness checklist; a config/secret presence matrix for the target; a migration review with backout notes; a deployment runbook with ordered steps; a post-deploy smoke-test script hitting each core page for status and timing; a rollback procedure; an on-call/escalation plan.
items.limitation: Risks: untested config drift between environments, non-additive migrations that cannot be backed out, releasing a build that differs from the tested artifact, missing secrets surfacing at runtime. Tests/validation: dry-run the runbook in staging; verify each core page returns quickly post-deploy (no hangs); verify rollback actually restores service; confirm health snapshot reads without blocking. Rollback/supportability: every step must have a defined reversal; keep the previous artifact and a DB restore point; never deploy a migration without an approved backout. Executive decision summary: 'Release is go only when build provenance, config, migrations (additive + reversible), smoke tests, and rollback are all green and owned.' Assumptions: staging mirrors production. Change-management/operations sign-off is required before go.

items.title: Analyze business process bottlenecks
items.description: Objective: locate where time, cost, and errors accumulate in a business process and recommend targeted interventions. Inputs required: the process steps and hand-offs; volume per step; cycle time and wait time per step; rework/error rates; who owns each step; system touchpoints; SLA targets. Analysis steps: 1) Map the as-is process end to end including queues and hand-offs. 2) Measure: capture cycle time vs. wait time per step (wait usually dominates). 3) Compute throughput and identify the constraint (the step that paces the whole flow). 4) Quantify rework loops and their cost. 5) Distinguish value-add from non-value-add steps (waiting, re-keying, approvals that rarely reject). 6) Hypothesize causes (capacity, batching, manual data entry, unclear ownership). 7) Propose interventions ranked by impact/effort and predict the new constraint after each fix.
items.applicability: Use when a process is 'too slow' or error-prone and leadership wants evidence-based targeting rather than blanket automation.
items.example: Outputs: an as-is process map with timings; a quantified bottleneck analysis identifying the constraint; a rework-cost estimate; a value-add vs. waste breakdown; a ranked intervention list (impact/effort) with the predicted next constraint; a target operating-metric set to track improvement.
items.limitation: Risks: optimizing a non-constraint (no end-to-end gain), measuring busy-time but not wait-time, anecdotal data, automating a broken process and scaling the error. Tests/validation: validate measurements against system logs/timestamps; pilot one intervention and re-measure; confirm the constraint actually moved. Rollback/supportability: introduce changes incrementally so each is reversible; keep baseline metrics to prove (or disprove) improvement. Executive decision summary: 'The constraint is step X; fixing it yields the largest cycle-time reduction; other steps are not worth optimizing yet.' Assumptions: timing data is representative of normal volume. Process-owner validation of the as-is map and measurements is required.

items.title: Convert a manual process into a digital workflow
items.description: Objective: turn a paper/email/spreadsheet manual process into a structured, auditable digital workflow without losing exception handling that humans currently do informally. Inputs required: the current manual steps; the artifacts exchanged (forms, emails, signatures); decision rules and their exceptions; volumes; the people and their roles; compliance/retention requirements. Analysis steps: 1) Document the as-is including the informal exceptions ('we just call Sarah when X'). 2) Model the to-be as an explicit state machine with roles, tasks, and SLAs. 3) Separate the happy path from exception paths and make exceptions first-class, not afterthoughts. 4) Define data capture (forms/validation) replacing free-text. 5) Design notifications, queues, and escalations. 6) Add audit and electronic approval/signature where required. 7) Plan change management: training, parallel run, and a fallback to manual during stabilization.
items.applicability: Use when a critical operation runs on email/paper, lacks an audit trail, and depends on tribal knowledge for exceptions, common in back-office banking ops.
items.example: Outputs: an as-is map including informal exceptions; a to-be workflow state machine with roles and SLAs; structured forms with validation replacing free text; queues, notifications, and escalation rules; an audit/e-approval design; a change-management and parallel-run plan.
items.limitation: Risks: digitizing only the happy path and breaking on the exceptions people quietly handled, removing human judgment where it is genuinely needed, change resistance, loss of an informal control. Tests/validation: walk every known exception through the new workflow; user-acceptance testing with the actual operators; parallel run before retiring the manual path. Rollback/supportability: keep the manual fallback available during stabilization; make exceptions visible and assignable, not stuck. Executive decision summary: 'Manual process becomes an auditable workflow with explicit exception handling; manual fallback retained until the digital path is proven.' Assumptions: all real exceptions were surfaced during discovery. Operations and compliance sign-off is required.

items.title: Design approval and exception handling
items.description: Objective: design robust approval routing and exception handling so nothing gets stuck, lost, or silently auto-approved. Inputs required: the items needing approval; routing rules (amount, type, risk); the authority hierarchy; delegation/out-of-office policy; SLA and escalation expectations; what counts as an exception vs. normal rejection. Analysis steps: 1) Define routing: static (by role) vs. dynamic (by attributes/thresholds). 2) Model approval outcomes: approve, reject, return-for-rework, escalate. 3) Design exception queues for items that fail routing (no eligible approver, conflict, missing data) so they surface instead of vanishing. 4) Define SLA timers and escalation chains; decide the policy when an SLA breaches (escalate, never auto-approve high-risk). 5) Handle delegation safely without collapsing segregation of duties. 6) Ensure idempotency and concurrency control so an item cannot be double-actioned. 7) Add full audit of every routing and decision event.
items.applicability: Use whenever approvals route across roles/thresholds and the business cannot tolerate stuck or silently approved items, payments, limits, onboarding, change requests.
items.example: Outputs: a routing-rule specification; an outcome state machine including rework and escalation; exception queues with ownership; SLA/escalation policies; a safe delegation design; concurrency/idempotency controls; an audit trail of routing and decisions.
items.limitation: Risks: auto-approval on SLA breach, items dead-ended with no eligible approver, delegation defeating four-eyes, double-approval races. Tests/validation: simulate no-eligible-approver and verify it lands in an exception queue; breach an SLA and verify escalation (not auto-approval); concurrent-approval race test; delegation-during-leave segregation test. Rollback/supportability: queued items must survive restarts; provide an admin reassignment path that is itself audited; never silently approve. Executive decision summary: 'Approvals route deterministically; exceptions surface to a queue; SLA breaches escalate, never auto-approve; every decision is audited.' Assumptions: authority hierarchy and SLAs are agreed. Compliance sign-off on escalation and delegation rules is required.

items.title: Plan a safe database migration
items.description: Objective: plan a database schema/data migration that is additive, reversible, and zero- or low-downtime, with no data loss. Inputs required: current and target schemas; data volumes and growth; downtime tolerance; the migration tooling (e.g., EF Core migrations); dependent applications; backup/restore capability; the change window. Analysis steps: 1) Classify each change as additive (safe) or destructive (drop/rename/lossy type change, requires approval). 2) Prefer the expand-contract pattern: add new structures, dual-write/backfill, switch reads, then later remove the old, never drop in the same release as the rename. 3) Estimate lock and runtime impact of each migration on production volumes; batch large backfills. 4) Define data validation: row counts, control totals, spot checks pre/post. 5) Define the backout for every step. 6) Sequence migrations and app deploys so old and new code both work during the transition. 7) Schedule within the approved window with a restore point.
items.applicability: Use for any non-trivial schema change on a live banking database where downtime or data loss is unacceptable and changes must be reversible.
items.example: Outputs: a change classification (additive vs. destructive); an expand-contract migration sequence; lock/runtime risk estimates with batching plan; a pre/post data-validation checklist (counts, control totals); per-step backout procedures; a coordinated migration + deploy schedule with a restore point.
items.limitation: Risks: destructive changes without backout, long-running migrations locking tables in business hours, schema changes that break older app instances mid-deploy, silent data loss on type changes. Tests/validation: run the migration against a production-sized copy and measure duration/locking; validate counts and control totals before and after; test backout restores prior state. Rollback/supportability: never combine a rename/drop with its dependent change in one release; keep a restore point; make backfills re-runnable and idempotent. Executive decision summary: 'Migration is additive and reversible, applied via expand-contract within the change window, with validated counts and a tested backout.' Assumptions: a production-sized test copy is available. Explicit human approval is required for any destructive change (per repo policy).

items.title: Create a test strategy for financial systems
items.description: Objective: define a risk-based test strategy for a financial system covering correctness of money math, controls, and auditability. Inputs required: the in-scope transactions and calculations; rounding/precision rules; the control points (approvals, limits, segregation); regulatory requirements; data-privacy constraints on test data; integration points. Analysis steps: 1) Identify the highest-risk areas: monetary calculations, rounding, interest/fees, reconciliations, approvals, and audit. 2) Define a test pyramid: unit tests for calculation and rule logic, integration tests for persistence and workflow, end-to-end for critical journeys. 3) Specify money-math tests: decimal precision, rounding direction, boundary amounts, negative/zero, currency edge cases. 4) Specify control tests: four-eyes enforcement, authority limits, audit completeness, IDOR. 5) Define reconciliation/control-total checks as tests. 6) Plan test data: anonymized/synthetic, never raw production PII. 7) Define regression and performance test scope and entry/exit criteria.
items.applicability: Use before building or releasing systems that move money or enforce financial controls, where a calculation or control defect has direct financial and regulatory impact.
items.example: Outputs: a risk-ranked test scope; a test-pyramid plan; a money-math test matrix (precision, rounding, boundaries); a controls test set (four-eyes, limits, audit, IDOR); reconciliation/control-total checks; a synthetic test-data plan; entry/exit criteria for release.
items.limitation: Risks: floating-point money errors, untested rounding rules, controls verified only in UI, using production PII in test, regression gaps. Tests/validation: assert exact decimal results, not approximate; boundary and negative-amount cases; prove controls fail closed when bypass is attempted; verify audit records exist for every state change. Rollback/supportability: maintain a regression suite that must pass before release; gate releases on control-test results. Executive decision summary: 'Testing is risk-based, proves money math and controls, uses synthetic data, and gates release on a passing controls + regression suite.' Assumptions: rounding/precision rules are documented and agreed. Finance/compliance sign-off on the money-math and control test sets is required.

items.title: Assess operational risk in a banking workflow
items.description: Objective: assess operational risk in a workflow and produce a prioritized mitigation plan. Inputs required: the workflow steps and controls; failure history/incidents; volumes and value at risk; manual touchpoints; key-person dependencies; system availability; the bank's risk appetite. Analysis steps: 1) Identify failure modes per step (people, process, system, external). 2) For each, estimate likelihood and impact (financial, regulatory, reputational) to rank risk. 3) Map existing controls (preventive, detective, corrective) to each failure mode and find gaps. 4) Identify single points of failure: key persons, single systems, manual re-keying, missing four-eyes. 5) Assess detectability: would a failure be noticed, and how fast. 6) Recommend mitigations ranked by residual-risk reduction vs. cost. 7) Define key risk indicators to monitor going forward.
items.applicability: Use when leadership or audit needs an evidence-based view of where a banking process could fail and what to fix first.
items.example: Outputs: a failure-mode register with likelihood/impact scoring; a control-coverage map with gaps; a single-point-of-failure list; a detectability assessment; a ranked mitigation plan by residual-risk reduction; a set of key risk indicators to monitor.
items.limitation: Risks: subjective scoring without data, focusing on rare catastrophic events while ignoring frequent small losses, controls that exist on paper but are not enforced, over-reliance on detection without prevention. Tests/validation: walk through recent incidents to validate the register catches them; verify each claimed control is actually enforced (e.g., test four-eyes bypass); sanity-check scoring with operators. Rollback/supportability: phase mitigations so each is reversible and measurable; track KRIs to confirm risk actually fell. Executive decision summary: 'Top operational risks are X, Y, Z (value at risk N); recommended mitigations cut residual risk most per unit cost.' Assumptions: incident history is reasonably complete. Risk/audit sign-off on scoring and the mitigation plan is required; this is not a substitute for the bank's formal risk framework.

items.title: Plan a rollout from pilot to production
items.description: Objective: plan a staged rollout from a controlled pilot to full production with clear gates and a credible rollback at every stage. Inputs required: pilot scope and success criteria; the full target population; risk tolerance; support capacity; training needs; the rollback mechanism; data-migration needs per stage. Analysis steps: 1) Define pilot success metrics up front (correctness, adoption, performance, support load) and the go/no-go threshold. 2) Choose a ramp strategy: by team, branch, transaction type, or percentage, lowest-risk cohort first. 3) Define gates between stages with explicit exit criteria. 4) Plan support and feedback capture during ramp. 5) Define rollback per stage (feature flag, routing, parallel run) so any stage can revert without data loss. 6) Plan training and comms per cohort. 7) Define the steady-state hand-over to support (runbooks, diagnostics, ownership).
items.applicability: Use when introducing a new system/process to a large user base where a big-bang rollout would be too risky and reversibility matters.
items.example: Outputs: pilot success criteria and go/no-go thresholds; a staged ramp plan by cohort; gate exit criteria; a per-stage rollback design; a support and feedback plan; training and comms per cohort; a steady-state support hand-over package.
items.limitation: Risks: declaring pilot success without measurable criteria, ramping too fast past warning signs, no rollback once data has diverged, support overwhelmed at scale. Tests/validation: verify pilot metrics are actually captured and meet thresholds before each gate; rehearse rollback at a stage boundary; load-test before the largest cohort. Rollback/supportability: every stage must be reversible without data loss; keep parallel run or feature flags until adoption is proven; hand support runbooks before, not after, full rollout. Executive decision summary: 'Rollout proceeds cohort by cohort behind explicit gates; each stage is reversible; full production only after pilot meets pre-agreed criteria.' Assumptions: cohorts can be isolated. Business-owner sign-off at each gate is required.

items.title: Design supportability diagnostics
items.description: Objective: make a system diagnosable in production so support can localize and resolve incidents quickly without a developer. Inputs required: the critical user journeys; current logging/telemetry; known recurring incidents; the support team's access and tooling; SLAs; the dependency map (DB, optional services). Analysis steps: 1) Define health endpoints that read a cached snapshot, never blocking on external services on the request path. 2) Add request timing/correlation so a request can be traced end to end and a hung endpoint is visible (started with no completed line). 3) Define structured logs with correlation ids, user, action, and outcome, no secrets/PII. 4) Build a small set of operational dashboards/queries for the top incident types. 5) Define self-checks: dependency reachability, queue depth, last-success timestamps, with graceful-degradation indicators. 6) Write runbooks linking symptoms to diagnostics to actions. 7) Define log retention and access controls.
items.applicability: Use when incidents take too long to diagnose, require a developer, or the system stalls without anyone knowing why, directly addresses the page-hang class of failures.
items.example: Outputs: non-blocking health endpoints backed by a cached snapshot; request timing + correlation logging that exposes hangs; structured PII-free logs; operational queries/dashboards for top incidents; dependency self-checks with degradation indicators; symptom-to-action runbooks; a log retention/access policy.
items.limitation: Risks: diagnostics that themselves block the request path (e.g., synchronous health probes causing hangs, explicitly forbidden), logging secrets/PII, noisy logs that hide signal, dashboards that query OLTP heavily. Tests/validation: induce a dependency outage and confirm pages still render from the cached snapshot; confirm a deliberately stalled endpoint is visible via the started/completed log gap; verify logs contain no secrets. Rollback/supportability: diagnostics must add no request-path latency; degrade gracefully when optional services are down. Executive decision summary: 'Production is diagnosable: health is read from a cache, hangs are visible in timing logs, and runbooks map symptoms to fixes, no developer required for first response.' Assumptions: support has appropriate access. Operations sign-off on runbooks and log policy is required.

items.title: Build a PDF summarizer with provenance
items.description: Objective: build a document summarizer that produces summaries traceable back to exact source passages, so users can verify every claim. Inputs required: representative documents (born-digital and scanned); the summary use case and audience; sensitivity/retention rules; available local model (optional); accuracy expectations. Analysis steps: 1) Build the extraction pipeline: detect text vs. scanned, extract text per page with page/coordinate anchors; route scanned pages to OCR with confidence scores. 2) Chunk by structure (sections/paragraphs) preserving the source anchor for each chunk. 3) Summarize per section, requiring each summary sentence to cite the chunk(s) it derives from (provenance). 4) Assemble a document summary that links every claim to its source location. 5) Flag low-OCR-confidence or low-coverage regions as 'not summarized reliably'. 6) Present summary with click-through to the highlighted source. 7) Degrade gracefully when no model is present (extractive fallback).
items.applicability: Use when users must trust and verify summaries of contracts, policies, or reports, banking will not accept unverifiable AI summaries.
items.example: Outputs: an extraction+OCR pipeline with page/coordinate anchors and confidence; structure-aware chunking with retained provenance; per-section summaries with citations to source passages; a coverage report flagging un-summarized/low-confidence regions; a UI linking each claim to highlighted source; an extractive fallback when no model is available.
items.limitation: Risks: hallucinated or unsupported claims, summaries that look authoritative but cite nothing, low-quality OCR silently feeding wrong text, missed pages presented as if complete. Do not claim the summary is a substitute for reading the source on legally or financially material documents. Tests/validation: verify every summary sentence resolves to a real source span; seed documents with known facts and check they are captured and cited; verify low-confidence regions are flagged, not silently summarized; coverage reconciliation (pages in vs. summarized). Rollback/supportability: keep originals; make provenance mandatory; surface confidence and coverage to the user. Executive decision summary: 'Summaries are verifiable, every claim links to its source, low-confidence regions are flagged, and the tool assists rather than replaces human review.' Assumptions: documents are within supported formats. Human review of summaries on material documents is required.

items.title: Build cheque OCR with human review
items.description: Objective: build a cheque-capture pipeline (amount, payee, date, MICR) that uses OCR/recognition for throughput but keeps humans in the loop for anything uncertain, never auto-posting low-confidence reads. Inputs required: representative cheque images (good and degraded); the fields to extract; the MICR/codeline format; courtesy vs. legal amount rules; clearing thresholds; acceptable error rate; volumes and SLA. Analysis steps: 1) Pre-process images (deskew, denoise, crop fields). 2) Recognize each field with a per-field confidence score; parse MICR codeline separately. 3) Cross-validate: courtesy amount (numeric) vs. legal amount (words) must agree; flag mismatch. 4) Apply confidence and cross-check thresholds: auto-accept only high-confidence, fully consistent reads; route everything else to human review. 5) Design the review UI showing the image beside the proposed values for fast correction. 6) Feed corrections back as quality signal and capture an error/exception trail. 7) Reconcile batch totals against expected control totals.
items.applicability: Use for cheque/document capture in clearing or deposit workflows where errors move real money and a wrong read must not auto-post.
items.example: Outputs: an image pre-processing + per-field recognition pipeline with confidence; MICR codeline parsing; courtesy-vs-legal amount cross-validation; a confidence-threshold routing policy (auto-accept vs. human review); a review/correction UI (image beside values); a correction/exception audit trail; batch control-total reconciliation.
items.limitation: Risks: auto-posting a misread amount, treating OCR confidence as truth, degraded/handwritten cheques, courtesy/legal mismatch ignored, throughput pressure tempting teams to widen auto-accept. Do not claim OCR replaces human verification for financial amounts; it reduces, not removes, manual review. Tests/validation: measure field-level accuracy on a labeled set; verify low-confidence and mismatched items are forced to review; confirm no auto-post above the configured risk threshold; reconcile batch totals. Rollback/supportability: keep cheque images; make thresholds configurable and conservative by default; every auto-accept and correction is audited; provide a kill switch to send all items to manual. Executive decision summary: 'OCR raises throughput on clear cheques; every uncertain or inconsistent read goes to a human; amounts are never auto-posted on low confidence.' Assumptions: a representative labeled sample exists to set thresholds. Operations sign-off on accuracy thresholds and the auto-accept policy is required.

items.title: Assess market forecasting model risk
items.description: Objective: assess the risk of relying on a market/financial forecasting model and define controls before it informs decisions. Inputs required: the model purpose and decisions it feeds; training data and its time range; features; the validation/backtest methodology; performance metrics; assumptions; who owns it; the model-risk-management policy. Analysis steps: 1) Clarify intended use and decision impact; a forecast that sizes risk limits needs stricter controls than an internal estimate. 2) Review data: representativeness, look-ahead bias, survivorship bias, regime coverage. 3) Review methodology: train/validation/test split integrity, out-of-time backtesting, overfitting checks. 4) Evaluate performance honestly: error metrics out-of-sample, behavior in stressed/regime-change periods, not just average accuracy. 5) Probe assumptions and stability: does it break when correlations shift. 6) Define ongoing controls: monitoring for drift, performance thresholds, and a human override. 7) Document limitations and the confidence bounds.
items.applicability: Use before a forecasting/ML model is allowed to influence financial decisions, where over-trusting a model that fails in a regime change causes real loss.
items.example: Outputs: an intended-use and decision-impact statement; a data-bias review (look-ahead, survivorship, regime coverage); a methodology/backtest critique; out-of-sample and stressed-period performance with error bounds; an assumptions-and-stability assessment; an ongoing monitoring/drift-control plan with thresholds and human override; a documented limitations statement.
items.limitation: Risks: overfitting that looks great in-sample, look-ahead/survivorship bias inflating accuracy, extrapolation beyond observed regimes, false precision, automation bias (humans deferring to the number). Markets are non-stationary; past performance does not guarantee future results and no forecast should be presented as certain. Tests/validation: out-of-time backtests; stress/regime-change scenarios; sensitivity to assumptions; data-leakage checks; ongoing drift monitoring against thresholds. Rollback/supportability: keep a human override and a fallback decision rule; monitor and retire/recalibrate on drift; never let the model auto-execute material decisions unattended. Executive decision summary: 'The model is useful as decision support within stated confidence bounds; it is not a guarantee, can fail in regime shifts, and requires monitoring plus human override.' Assumptions: validation data is representative. Formal model-risk-management review and human sign-off are required; this is not investment advice.

items.title: Create an executive recommendation memo
items.description: Objective: turn analysis into a crisp, decision-ready executive memo that a non-technical leader can act on. Inputs required: the problem statement; the analysis/findings; the options considered; cost/effort/risk per option; the recommended option and rationale; constraints; the decision being requested. Analysis steps: 1) Lead with the recommendation and the decision needed (BLUF, bottom line up front). 2) State the problem and why it matters now (impact, cost of inaction). 3) Present 2-4 options with honest trade-offs (cost, time, risk, benefit), including the do-nothing option. 4) Justify the recommendation against the options. 5) State key risks and how they are mitigated. 6) Give a phased plan with milestones, owners, and decision gates. 7) Quantify expected outcome and how success will be measured; keep it to one to two pages with detail in an appendix.
items.applicability: Use to convert any technical analysis or playbook output into something leadership can approve, fund, and own, the bridge between engineering analysis and an executive decision.
items.example: Outputs: a one-to-two-page memo with bottom-line-up-front recommendation; a clear problem and impact statement; an options table with trade-offs including do-nothing; the recommendation and rationale; key risks with mitigations; a phased plan with milestones, owners, and gates; success metrics; a technical appendix.
items.limitation: Risks: burying the recommendation, false confidence/over-claiming certainty, hiding the do-nothing option, omitting risks, unquantified benefits, recommending the technically interesting over the commercially sensible. Tests/validation: have a non-technical reader confirm they can make the decision from page one; verify every claimed number traces to the analysis; confirm risks and assumptions are stated, not hidden. Rollback/supportability: frame the plan in reversible phases with decision gates so commitment is staged, not all-or-nothing. Executive decision summary: the memo itself is the executive decision summary, one line: recommend option X to achieve outcome Y at cost Z, decision needed by date D. Assumptions and dependencies must be stated explicitly. Final accountability and sign-off rest with the human decision-maker.

items.title: Integrate a legacy system via an anti-corruption layer
items.description: Objective: integrate a new application with a legacy banking system (BDM/MCIB/ETAMS-style) without letting the legacy model leak into and corrupt the new design. Inputs required: the legacy interfaces (files, DB tables, stored procs, queues, screen-scrape); their data model and quirks; throughput and timing constraints; ownership and change-freeze status of the legacy system; failure/retry behavior; reconciliation requirements. Analysis steps: 1) Catalogue the integration surface and its semantics (including undocumented quirks and error codes). 2) Define a clean domain model for the new system independent of the legacy shape. 3) Build an anti-corruption layer (adapter) that translates between the legacy model and the new domain, isolating quirks in one place. 4) Decide integration style: batch file exchange, DB-level, message/queue, or API facade, prefer loose coupling and async where the legacy is fragile. 5) Design idempotency, retries with backoff, and dead-letter handling for legacy flakiness. 6) Add reconciliation/control totals across the boundary. 7) Define monitoring so boundary failures surface immediately.
items.applicability: Use when a new system must talk to an old, possibly unchangeable, core banking system and you must protect the new design and the data integrity across the boundary.
items.example: Outputs: an integration-surface catalogue with semantics and quirks; a clean new-domain model; an anti-corruption adapter isolating legacy translation; a chosen integration style with idempotency/retry/dead-letter design; cross-boundary reconciliation and control totals; boundary monitoring and alerting.
items.limitation: Risks: legacy data model bleeding into the new system, tight coupling making both brittle, duplicate or lost messages on retry, silent boundary failures, change freezes on the legacy side blocking fixes. Tests/validation: contract tests against the legacy interface (or a faithful stub); idempotency tests (replay the same message, expect one effect); failure-injection on the boundary; reconciliation proving nothing is lost or duplicated. Rollback/supportability: keep the adapter the single point of legacy knowledge so changes are localized; design flows to be replayable; monitor and alert on boundary health. Executive decision summary: 'The new system integrates through an isolating adapter, stays decoupled from legacy quirks, and reconciles every transaction across the boundary.' Assumptions: legacy interface behavior can be observed or documented. Sign-off from the legacy-system owner on the integration contract is required.
